                                                    Perspectives in Ecology and Conservation
                                                                 journal homepage: www.perspectecolconserv.com


Policy Forums

Beyond Boundaries: Rethinking Biomes Interdependencies in
Conservation Policies
Fabio de Oliveira Roque a,b,i, Charlotte Ndiribe a,c , Gerhard E. Overbeck b,d ,
José Henrique F. Mello b,e , Mariana G. Bender f, Helena G. Bergallo g ,
Ronaldo Christofoletti h , Guarino R. Colli b,i , Juliano J. Corbi j,
Geraldo A. Damasceno Junior k, Michele S. Dechoum l,
Adrian A. Garda b,m, Carlos Eduardo V. Grelle b,e,
Aretha F. Guimaraes n , Leandro Juen o , Inara R. Leal p ,
Guilherme O. Longo q , Fernando L. Mantelatto r ,
Luciano N. Naka s , André Valle Nunes b,t, Jose Manuel Ochoa Quintero u,

2530-0644/© 2025 Associação Brasileira de Ciência Ecológica e Conservação. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND

    Please cite this article as: Fabio de Oliveira Roque et al., , https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pecon.2025.12.001
F.O. Roque et al.                                                                                               Perspectives in Ecology and Conservation xxx (xxxx) xxx



H I G H L I G H T S                             G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T

• Current policies isolate biomes, over­
  looking        crucial       ecological
  interdependencies
• Biomes are linked by cross-boundary
  processes like water cycles and
  migrations
• Isolated biome approaches limit con­
  servation success and ecological
  understanding
• An integrated strategy recognizing
  biome links is needed for robust
  conservation




A R T I C L E I N F O                           A B S T R A C T

Keywords:                                       Biomes are fundamental units for biodiversity conservation given their large geographical coverage and pecu­
Biodiversity management                         liarities. The interconnectivity among biomes maintains biodiversity and climate patterns at larger spatial and
Brazil                                          temporal scales. Therefore, treating biomes as isolated entities can undermine the effectiveness of environmental
Conservation strategies
                                                policies and governance towards sustainability goals. In Brazil, for instance, such fractional perspectives could
Ecosystem interconnectivity
Sociobiodiversity
                                                create fragmented or limited understanding of important socio-ecological interconnections among biomes, ul­
Socioecology.                                   timately constraining effective conservation strategies and policies. Yet much has been done to integrate Bra­
                                                zilian biomes in scientific research or conservation projects, here we portray how the adoption of an integrated
                                                view that considers the synergies and interdependencies among biomes is critical for promoting effective con­
                                                servation. This is important to ensure the persistence of significant ecological benefits derived from the natural
                                                resources of these biomes.




    In the current world where policies are increasingly retrogressive            maintenance of biodiversity and ecosystem services across biomes.
with lapse economic and environmental protection measures, a thor­                However, current legislation does not adequately account for
ough understanding of ecological interconnections has become imper­               cross-biome interactions or for the disproportionate effects that con­
ative to safeguarding biodiversity from rapid decline. Policies, nature           servation actions in one biome can have on another. Similarly, many
management and conservation initiatives worldwide often treat biomes              research institutions and non-governmental organizations (NGOs)
as isolated entities, overlooking their interconnectedness and the                operate within biome-specific mandates (e.g. the National Institutes of
broader ecological processes that transcend boundaries (Fernandes                 Amazonia, Pantanal, Atlantic Forest, Caatinga, and Cerrado). This
et al., 2023). Specifically, in Brazil, conservation policies and research        fragmented focus raises several important questions. For instance, when
initiatives are mainly structured around the country´s officially desig­          will research and policy agendas finally move beyond biome-specific
nated biomes, as defined by the Brazilian Institute of Geography and              frameworks to embrace integrated, cross-biome approaches? And
Statistics (IBGE, 2019), linked to the Planning and Budget Ministry of            when will the relevant authorities establish a National Institute dedi­
the Brazilian government (Box 1). This connection reflects the strategic          cated to cross-biome biodiversity research and conservation?
role of this institute (IBGE) in producing essential statistical and                  Educational materials also contribute to this fragmented under­
geographical data for planning and formulating public policies. The               standing. Textbooks and reference works commonly organize content
biome-based perspective is a useful and operational reference scale for           around individual biomes, reinforcing a compartmentalized view of
landscape planning, geopolitical identity, and ecological assessments,            evolutionary, biogeographical, and ecological relationships. A quick

often focus excessively on biome uniqueness, overlooking their complex            sive syntheses of Brazilian biodiversity tend to follow this biome-based
interconnections. Less attention is given to the reality that biomes are          structure. While such classifications have didactic and even dialectical
not homogenous entities, but rather large compositions of numerous,               value—highlighting both similarities and differences—they also inad­
diverse ecosystems with distinct characteristics that often evade clear           vertently reinforce rigid conceptual boundaries. Consequently, the
delimitations. Regional biodiversity does not always conform to defin­            public is largely unaware that many species occur in more than one

example, the Brazilian law for the Protection of Native Vegetation es­            ecological processes. This is exemplified with the analyses of several
tablishes biome-specific regulations but overlooks the ecological con­            plant species that portray broad ranges and distributions across Brazil
nections among biomes, which call for conservation strategies focused             (Flora e Funga do Brasil, 2025), as well as bird species, of which
on addressing the sources of environmental impacts rather than their              approximately 10% are migratory (Somenzari et al., 2018). A notable
isolated effects (Marques et al., 2020). The Cerrado biome, notably,              example is that of two quite “distinct” Brazilian biomes, the Caatinga
serves as the source of numerous Brazilian river basins, which merge              and Cerrado, which share approximately 60% of their bird species (Lima
further downstream with larger systems situated in adjacent biomes                et al., 2022). In this case, scientific knowledge or documented long-term
(Oliveira et al., 2014). Thus, a more conservative protection of the ri­          records on local migration, particularly regarding seasonality and
parian vegetation within the Cerrado is crucial for the conservation and          foraging, remain scarce. In the marine realm, the concept of a biome is


                                                                              2
F.O. Roque et al.                                                                                               Perspectives in Ecology and Conservation xxx (xxxx) xxx




   Box 1
   Brazilian biomes

   Brazil has six officially recognized biomes (IBGE, 2019): Amazon, Atlantic Forest, Caatinga, Cerrado, Pampa and Pantanal. These biomes (sensu
   IBGE) also correspond to the major phytogeographic regions of the country. The corresponding biomes in an international biome classification
   (Dinerstein et al., 2017) are ‘Tropical Moist Broadleaf Forests’ (including IBGE's Amazon, Atlantic Forest), ‘Tropical and subtropical Dry
   Broadleaf Forests’ (Caatinga), ‘Tropical and Subtropical Grasslands, Savannas and Shrublands’ (Cerrado, Pampa) and ‘Flooded Grasslands and
   Savannas’ (Pantanal). Note that the two classification systems differ concerning biome boundaries, reinforcing that biome-based conservation
   strategies can be problematic, as they depend on the details of specific classifications. In addition, the country is slowly moving towards officially
   recognizing eight distinct coastal-marine ecosystems in its Exclusive Economic Zone (Magris et al., 2021), which is central to advance our
   understanding on the ecological interconnections between land and sea ecological processes and develop better-informed conservation policies.




even more elusive, as the distinctions and potential “boundaries” be­               planning based on functional connectivity and adequate habitat condi­
tween marine ecosystems are concealed beneath the water's surface,                  tions for achieving biodiversity conservation goals and tackling climate
beyond human sight. Yet there are still diverse marine ecosystems and               change. Moreover, within-biome divisions further aggravate the prob­
their habitats, such as coastal biomes, coral and rocky reefs, mesophotic           lems, as large interconnected systems are often divided into independent
reefs, the deep and open oceans, not to mention the polar seas (Box 1).             units (Araújo et al., 2022). Additionally, funding calls for conservation
Within coastal biomes, mangroves and estuaries act as crucial bridges,              and research projects on biodiversity tend to be structured around
not only between terrestrial and marine environments but also among                 specific biome-based objectives or geopolitical boundaries, further
marine ecosystems. They foster these connections through biogeo­                    entrenching these divisions. Some large-scale research initiatives, such
chemical cycles, species' life cycles, ecosystem processes, and the social          as the Programa de Pesquisa em Biodiversidade (PPBio - Brazilian
and cultural services they provide. Because the boundaries and pro­                 Biodiversity Research Program), and Programa de Pesquisas Ecológicas
cesses of coastal-marine ecosystems are fluid and multidimensional,                 de Longa Duração (PELD - Long Term Ecological Research) also exem­
these environments often remain excluded from biome-driven policies,                plify these divisions (Rosa et al., 2021). Despite the evident success of
even though this reflects an inherent challenge of marine classification            both Programs, they also started with a focus on terrestrial biomes while

numerous benefits they provide to human populations from nutritional                PELD Program (Cordeiro et al., 2022). Coastal-marine ecosystems were

biome boundaries. Deforestation across Amazonia, for example, has                   regions are ecologically and socioeconomically linked, and “aerial
already reduced annual rainfall across large areas of Brazil and other              rivers”, where moisture from one biome impacts precipitation patterns
parts of South America (Bottino et al., 2024). Similarly, in 2024, smoke            in another, critically illustrate the deep interconnections among biomes
from fires in Amazonia, Cerrado and Pantanal affected human pop­                    (Arraut et al., 2012). Furthermore, there are significant hydrological
ulations and ecosystems in the Atlantic Forest and the Pampa biome                  dependencies, socio-ecological spillover effects, and animal migrations
(Monteiro dos Santos et al., 2024). In the same way, the ocean plays a              across the boundaries of these biomes. Some studies have even drawn
fundamental role in the interconnection of terrestrial biomes, particu­             attention to the risks that strict deforestation limits in a biome, for
larly through the water cycle. Recent record-high ocean temperatures                instance, may unintentionally shift deforestation pressures to adjacent
have disrupted hydrological cycles, altering precipitation patterns and             ones (Kuschnig et al., 2021; Fernandes et al., 2023). Interconnections

et al., 2021; Santos et al., 2023). These changes directly affect terrestrial       terrestrial-aquatic systems, terrestrial, freshwater, and marine systems)
biomes, modifying their functioning and exacerbating environmental                  are still currently understudied. It highlights the necessity for an inte­
stressors such as droughts and wildfires. Moisture transport from the               grative approach to merge biomes within a larger connected research
ocean, including aerial rivers that carry humidity across continents, is a          and conservation framework.
crucial component of cross-biome interactions and should be considered                  While the permeability and ecological interdependence of biome
in conservation and climate adaptation strategies. Cross-biome in­                  boundaries are widely acknowledged within the scientific community
fluences are particularly pronounced in ecotones. Here, shared animal               (e.g., Gosz and Sharpe, 1989; Ferro and Morrone, 2014, and the above
and plant species, as well as joint environmental characteristics create            references), this understanding has not yet been fully reflected in
unique mosaic landscapes and environments with multiple influences                  research practices and policy frameworks. In practice, many biogeo­
that demand specific conservation approaches (Kark, 2007). Even at the              graphic classifications, large-scale assessments, and conservation pro­
global level, there are clear examples of biomes and ecoregions that                grams continue to use biome limits as fixed reference units, reinforcing
influence one another, such as the Sahara desert’s dust, which fertilizes           the perception of rigid boundaries. This persistence creates a form of
and increases productivity in the Amazon (Bristow et al., 2010).                    closed circular loop, in which conceptual simplifications are institu­
    In advocating for a conservation approach that transcends biome                 tionalized through policies and monitoring frameworks and subse­
boundaries, it is important to recognize that each biome faces high, yet            quently reabsorbed into scientific analyses. Consequently, the challenge
different, levels of anthropogenic pressure and conservation urgency.               extends beyond institutional or operational barriers—it also involves
The Amazon Rainforest, for instance, harbors the most species-rich and              epistemological and systemic feedbacks between scientific paradigms
biodiverse ecosystems on Earth. It provides essential ecosystem services            and governance structures. This dynamic, reminiscent of Kuhn’s (1962)
at regional and global scales, yet continues to experience one of the               notion of paradigm persistence, helps explain why, despite robust sci­
highest global deforestation rates (Bottino et al., 2024). This persistent          entific evidence of ecological interconnectivity, biome-based thinking
threat is exacerbated by a history of inadequate funding for Amazonian              remains deeply rooted in conservation planning and environmental
research, limiting the comprehension of its ecological systems                      management.
(Guimaraes et al., 2024). Conservation efforts have also failed to fully                Given these complexities, we are not advocating for the decon­
engage local communities, Indigenous peoples, and Amazonian in­                     struction of the biome-based approach, but rather highlighting the need
stitutions, whose knowledge is crucial for sustainable strategies                   for more cross-biome initiatives, as well as the incorporation of social
(Fernández-Llamazares et al., 2021). While external expertise is valu­             and environmental biome interconnectivity measures that are more
able, initiatives must prioritize the contributions of those who have lived         explicit into conservation strategies. Recognizing and addressing the
in and cared for the territory for generations, ensuring their voices in            aforementioned linkages among systems can yield robust and resilient
shaping decisions. Another example, the South Brazilian Pampa region                approaches to biodiversity conservation. On this premise, we outline
is often overlooked in conservation, even though its high conversion and            seven practical steps to achieve an integrated vision of cross-biome
low coverage of protected areas makes it highly threatened (Overbeck                conservation: 1) Redesign or Improve Organizational Structures:
et al., 2015). Biome awareness disparities persist in the conservation              Conservation organizations currently focused on single biomes should
debate (Silveira et al., 2022; Overbeck et al., 2024). A well-balanced              expand their scope to include inter-biome connections. This could
conservation strategy should not divert critical resources from                     involve forming interdisciplinary and transdisciplinary teams capable of
high-priority areas, rather it should enhance their protection by rein­             addressing social-ecological linkages across biomes and ecosystems. 2)
forcing their ecological and sociobiological interdependencies with                 Funding for Interconnected Systems: Research and conservation
adjacent biomes. Likewise, research on zoonotic diseases, such as the               funding should emphasize projects that explore cross-biome in­
hantaviruses, should be extended across biomes to increase the                      terdependencies and relationships. Investments should support initia­
comprehension of transmission dynamics and ecological drivers (Mello                tives that protect ecological corridors, river networks, ocean-terrestrial
et al., 2024). Coastal-marine ecosystems also suffer from lack of                   connections and key transition areas such as mangroves, estuaries,
appropriate funding both for research and to face the challenges of                 coastal sand dune vegetation, riparian zones, mountain chains, and
implementing effective conservation actions given its size and logistical           migratory paths. 3) Research Institute Collaboration and Mobility:
constraints (Magris et al., 2021). Acknowledging the ocean’s role in                Teams in biome-focused research institutes could increase mobility and
planetary regulation and its links to terrestrial biomes, the United Na­            foster collaborations across biomes. Also, expanding programs, such as
tions launched the Decade of Ocean Science for Sustainable Develop­                 Brazil’s PELD and PPBio, should include cross-biome research initiatives
ment (2021–2030) (Ryabinin et al., 2019) to promote cross-system                    that can facilitate knowledge exchange and holistic ecological assess­
research and foster conservation and sustainable use.                               ments. In this regard, improving cross-border collaborations among
    Empirical evidence has increasingly revealed that biomes are not                South American countries is essential. Fortunately, initiatives, such as
separated by hard boundaries (Moncrieff et al., 2016), and often,                   those among Amazonian nations represent steps in this direction. Other
ecological studies are conducted across their official limits. For example,         closely-knit ecosystems, such as the Pantanal and Chaco can adopt their
the grasslands Pampas in southern Brazil, extend into the southern part             success strategies, most probably through significant investments in
of the Atlantic Forest biome (Marques and Grelle, 2021; Overbeck et al.,            cooperative research and conservation efforts. 4) Legislative and Pol­

legal processes abound across biomes. This new approach will require a            committees or Indigenous Territorial Management Plans that already
well-designed co-production process for regulations, ensuring that cross-         operate across multiple biomes. Within academia, the transition also
biome initiatives incorporate differential implementations based on the           entails making these topics more explicit in disciplinary and interdisci­
interconnections between biomes. 5) Education and Training: Teach­                plinary curricula, incorporating active learning approaches that enable
ing materials for all ages, including textbooks, should contain instructive       students to engage with cross-biome problem-solving in practical,
content on biome interdependencies. For instance, courses in biogeog­             context-sensitive ways.
raphy, ecology, conservation biology, and sociobiodiversity should                    It is important to highlight that despite our primary focus on the
incorporate inter-biome dynamics. This would start from the insertion of          conservation of Brazilian biomes, it is expedient to consider the steps
this discussion both at schools and academic-level courses to prepare             outlined above for adoption in conservation across other biomes and

and co-production are fundamental to transforming concepts and ap­                research collaborations are crucial for comprehensive exchange of ideas

Research Questions: Research efforts should begin to address critical             example is the Chaco, which despite not being recognized as a Brazilian
questions and solutions to conservation problems in the context of cross-         biome is important for other countries in South America. The Amazon,
biomes systems, such as the biogeochemical cycles, traditional knowl­             located in a large part of the national territory and in lands of neigh­
edge systems, species migrations, evolutionary and ecological processes           boring countries, such as Guiana, Suriname, Venezuela, Ecuador,
across biomes and ecotones. Programs should prioritize studies on                 Colombia, Peru and Bolivia is another example of a transboundary
spillover effects, system's connectivity (e.g. hydrological and biodiver­         challenge. The Atlantic countries are discussing an international
sity fluxes), and telecoupling impacts to inform policies aligning the            agreement of the All-Atlantic Ocean Research and Innovation Alliance
interconnected dynamics of biomes. 7) Social Actors Involvement in                (https://allatlanticocean.org/about/) that can inspire multinational
Cross-Biome Initiatives: Effective cross-biome conservation requires              partnerships for other biomes. In conclusion, an integrated and inter­
the inclusion of all social actors—local communities, Indigenous peo­             connected approach accounting for interdependencies among biomes
ples, researchers and policymakers. Traditional, local and Indigenous             has potentials to advance biodiversity conservation on a global scale.
knowledge is essential for creating culturally-appropriate and socially-          This will foster the development of effective and sustainable strategies
equitable conservation strategies. Engaging these stakeholders fosters            around the complex socio-ecological and dynamic relationships among
collaboration, enhances knowledge transfer, and ensures conservation              biomes. Through considerations for the cross-biome approach, re­
efforts are scientifically grounded and context relevant. This holistic           searchers can promote robust, scalable, and resilient conservation ac­
approach is key to addressing the interconnected ecological challenges            tions, with higher weight given to biodiversity and ecosystem protection
across biomes. 8) Enhance the Visibility of Cross-Biome Initiatives:              for the benefit of future generations.
Building a shared sense of belonging around the idea of interconnected
biomes can strengthen public support for integrated conservation. Brazil          Declaration of competing interest
already hosts several protected areas located in transitional zones, such
as "Refúgio da Serra dos Montes Altos" (Caatinga-Cerrado transition),                 The authors declare that they have no known competing financial

areas could be facilitated by identifying priority zones where socio-                  approach to protecting half the terrestrial realm. BioScience 67, 534–545. https://
ecological processes are particularly vulnerable, land-use pressures are               doi.org/10.1093/biosci/bix014.
intensifying, and climate change impacts are strongest. Likewise, rec­            Fernandes, G.W., Oliveira, H.F., Bergallo, H.G., Borges-Junior, V.N., Colli, G.,

ommendations for stakeholder participation should address strategies                   costs of Europe’s deforestation policy. Science 379, 341–342. https://doi.org/
for reconciling competing priorities, drawing lessons from river basin                 10.1126/science.adg2983.
